text:
Survival  analysis  focuses  on the  estimation of  time-to-event
distributions while considering the effects of  censoring. Over
time,  this field has witnessed substantial  progress,  initially
driven by  conventional  statistical  approaches  like  the Cox
proportional  hazards model  (Cox,  1972).  However,  inherent
limitations  and challenges within  this  domain have  spurred
the integration of machine learning methodologies,  introducing
enhanced performance and flexibility (Wang et al., 2019). This
convergence of  statistics and machine  learning  is particularly
evident in the R programming language (R Core Team, 2022),
where various frameworks have been developed to facilitate the

ntelligence, interpretable machine learning
application of both traditional  statistical models and modern
machine learning techniques for survival analysis tasks.
Despite the promising potential of integrating machine learning
into biomedical  research and healthcare,  the opaque nature
of black-box models has raised valid concerns (Ahmad et al.,
2018). In response, interpretable machine learning and explainable
artificial  intelligence methods has emerged as a viable solution
(Biecek and Burzykowski, 2021; Molnar, 2022), including software
packages  in R (Biecek,  2018; Molnar et al.,  2018). However,
these software packages cannot handle censoring and do not
provide  explanations  for  survival models.  To fill  this  gap,
we propose  survex as  an  innovative  solution that provides
comprehensive  explanations  for  entire models  and  individual
1

In the domain of  survival analysis,  various software packages  sup
offer a range of  statistical and machine  learning methods.  In  ma
R, an essential component is the survival package (Therneau,
2023), which contains  the  fundamental  statistical models  like
3.
the Cox proportional hazards (Cox, 1972) and accelerated failure
time (Kalbfleisch and Prentice, 2002) models. Alongside this,  Th
randomForestSRC (Ishwaran and Kogalur, 2007) and ranger  fun
(Wright and Ziegler, 2017) packages provide implementations of  me
the notable random survival  forest algorithm (Ishwaran et al.,  Su
2008).  Complementing  these,  the  survivalmodels  package
(Sonabend, 2022) offers deep neural networks within the survival  pa
analysis paradigm. To streamline consistency within this diverse  sur
toolkit, frameworks such as mlr3proba (Sonabend et al., 2021)  int
and censored (Hvitfeldt and Frick,  2023)  extend the mlr3  (20
(Lang et al., 2019) and parsnip (Kuhn and Vaughan, 2023)  mo
frameworks, respectively, to provide standardized ways of using  an
different survival models.  as
To  illustrate the wide range of model  explainability tools  ret
available  in the R environment,  it  is pertinent  to highlight  ha
packages such as DALEX (Biecek, 2018) and iml (Molnar et al.,
2018), which offer a diverse spectrum of XAI techniques. While  com
there are many packages in this field,  their core focus remains  as
rooted in the domain of explaining classification and regression  int
models. Adapting certain explanatory methods for survival models  ob
can be accomplished, but it requires careful adjustment due to  use
the unique predictive nature of  these models. To address this  pro

llenge,  survex offers specifically tailored explanations that
orporate the time dimension inherent in the survival models’
dictions. Furthermore, methods dedicated to explain survival
dels have been formulated, such as SurvLIME (Kovalev et al.,
0) with a Python implementation (Pach´on-Garc´ıa et al., 2024)
SurvSHAP(t) (Krzyzin´ski et al., 2023). The survex package is
ipped to incorporate also these advanced techniques,  further
ancing its  functionalities range.  It  is worth acknowledging
inspiration drawn  from the  survxai  package  (Grudziaz
al., 2018), which has significantly influenced the development
survex.  However,  survex offers  a broader  spectrum of
ctionalities,  recently proposed explanation methods,  and
ports a wider range of models. In addition, survxai is no longer
intained.

e  following  section  presents  a  brief  overview  of  the
ctionalities of  survex. More details about the explanation
thods and implementation can be found in the accompanying
pplementary Information and package documentation.
The design of survex draws inspiration from the DALEX R
ckage (Biecek, 2018), serving as an extension tailored to explain
vival models. This is exemplified in a methodically structured
erface, aligned with the structure proposed by Baniecki et al.
23a). The package  facilitates the  interpretation of  survival
dels through its diverse functionalities, categorized into local
d global contexts, as depicted in Figure 1. survex is engineered
a model-agnostic  framework adaptable to any model  that
urns predictions in the form of a survival function or cumulative
zards function.
The model-agnostic approach is implemented by the central
ponent  of  the package –  the explainer object.  Serving
a wrapper  for  survival models,  it unifies  their prediction
erfaces and stores  essential  background data necessary  for
taining predictions and explanations. For models from widely-
d packages,  the explainer can be created automatically, by
viding the model object to the explain() function. However,

Currently,  survex handles  the most  common case of  right-
censored data with a single type of event. However,  a future
roadmap envisions  its extension to cover alternative censoring
types and accommodate competing risk models. Moreover, survex
can be easily extended with additional explanation techniques,
e.g.,  counterfactual  explanations proposed by Kovalev et al.
(2021),  or more adaptations of  existing methods known from
classification and regression tasks.
Supplementary data
Supplementary data are available at Bioinformatics online.
